title: Shape a service Config
scenario: A tiny service needs configuration that can be owned by the service and moved between setup functions.
instructions: Define Config with owned fields: service_url: String, max_connections: usize, and use_tls: bool.

files.content: // TODO: define Config with owned fields for the service settings.

hints.body: Use a struct when a few named values travel together.
String is appropriate when Config owns its service URL.
Booleans are fine for small binary flags such as TLS on or off.

completionExplanation: The Config type gives the service one explicit setup object. Owned fields make the value easy to store without tying it to temporary input lifetimes.

title: Design a parse error enum
scenario: A small user parser needs a typed error instead of string errors. Today you are defining the public error type that the rest of the parser arc will build on.
instructions: Replace the TODO with ParseUserError variants for the parser cases below: MissingId, MissingName, MissingEmail, and InvalidId.

hints.body: The parser already names every failure case it wants to return.
Each distinct failure mode should usually become its own enum variant.
Keep these as programmatic enum variants; human-facing text comes later with Display.

completionExplanation: Each parser failure now has a named enum variant. Empty fields and invalid IDs become typed results that callers can match on instead of parsing strings or comparing error messages.

title: Define an inventory item
scenario: Inventory summaries need a small domain type before helpers can work with names and quantities.
instructions: Define Item with sku: String, name: String, quantity: u32, and reserved: u32.

files.content: // TODO: define the Item struct used by inventory summaries.

hints.body: Use a struct when each value has a distinct role.
The user-facing name and SKU are owned strings.
Quantities are small non-negative counts, so u32 is enough here.
hints.solutionCode:
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub struct Item {
    pub sku: String,
    pub name: String,
    pub quantity: u32,
    pub reserved: u32,
}

completionExplanation: The Item struct gives later helpers a clear vocabulary. Named fields make summary code easier to read than tuple positions.

title: Borrow fields in a log entry
scenario: A log viewer often points into existing log text instead of allocating new strings for every parsed line.
instructions: Define LogEntry<'a> with borrowed level: &'a str and message: &'a str fields.

files.content: // TODO: define LogEntry with borrowed fields.

hints.body: Borrowed struct fields need a lifetime parameter on the struct.
Both fields can borrow string slices from the original log line.
Use &'a str for each borrowed text field.

completionExplanation: Borrowed fields let the entry view point into existing text. The lifetime parameter states that the entry cannot outlive the data it references.

title: Define the Request surface
scenario: A small client library needs a request type that can be passed around after setup.
instructions: Define Request with method: String, path: String, and body: Option<String>.

files.content: // TODO: define the Request type used by the client API.

hints.body: Keep the request data owned so it can outlive the builder.
The body may be absent, which makes Option a good fit.
Use clear field names that match the API vocabulary.

completionExplanation: A focused Request struct gives the API a stable output type. Owned fields keep the request independent from temporary builder input.

title: Give Config sensible defaults
scenario: Most local service runs use the same setup. Default lets callers start from a reasonable Config and override only what changes.
instructions: Implement Default for Config. Use http://localhost:8080, 32 max connections, and false for use_tls.

hints.body: Default returns Self from fn default() -> Self.
Use to_owned or String::from for the service URL.
Derive would use String::new and 0. Implement Default manually because this lesson needs local development values.

completionExplanation: Default gives callers a clear starting point while keeping each field explicit. It is useful when most configuration values are optional overrides.

title: Format parse errors for people
scenario: Callers can match on ParseUserError, but logs and command-line output still need clear human-readable messages.
instructions: Implement std::fmt::Display for ParseUserError. Use a match on self and write a short message for each variant.

hints.body: Display is implemented with fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result.
A match on self keeps one message next to each error case.
Use write!(f, "...") inside each match arm.

completionExplanation: Display separates the typed error representation from the text shown to users. The enum stays useful for programs, while fmt gives each case a stable message.

title: Collect available item names
scenario: A dashboard only needs names for items whose available count is above zero.
instructions: Implement available_names with filter_map. Include an item name only when quantity is greater than reserved.

hints.body: available stock is quantity minus reserved, but compare before subtracting.
filter_map can return Some(name) for included items and None for skipped items.
Borrow names with as_str instead of cloning them.

completionExplanation: filter_map fits this helper because filtering and mapping happen together. It keeps the output borrowed from the input slice instead of allocating new strings.

title: Create a lifetime-backed view
scenario: A log view should borrow a slice of entries and expose helper methods without taking ownership.
instructions: Define LogView<'a> with entries: &'a [LogEntry<'a>].

hints.body: The view borrows the slice, so the field starts with &'a.
The entries inside the slice also carry the same lifetime.
This is a view type, not an owning collection.

completionExplanation: The view type captures the relationship between the borrowed slice and the borrowed text inside each entry. The lifetime keeps those references tied together.

title: Add consuming builder setters
scenario: A request builder should support chained calls without exposing all its intermediate fields.
instructions: Keep the Request type, then add RequestBuilder with optional method and path fields. Implement RequestBuilder::new() -> Self and RequestBuilder::method(mut self, method: impl Into<String>) -> Self.

hints.body: Taking mut self works well for chainable builder setters.
impl Into<String> accepts both borrowed and owned string inputs.
Return self after updating the field.

completionExplanation: Consuming setters make the builder ergonomic while preserving ownership. Each call returns the next builder state for the chain.

title: Add a small Config setter
scenario: Callers should be able to start from Config::default and customize one field without reaching into every detail.
instructions: Add Config::with_service_url(self, service_url: impl Into<String>) -> Self. Update the field and return self.

hints.body: A consuming setter can take mut self, change a field, and return self.
impl Into<String> lets callers pass either String or &str.
Returning Self keeps the method chain-friendly.

completionExplanation: A small method can make configuration ergonomic without hiding the Config fields. Taking self by value is a common builder-style pattern.

title: Mark the parse error as an Error
scenario: The parser error now has Display. Implementing the standard Error trait lets it fit into normal Rust error handling APIs.
instructions: Add an implementation of std::error::Error for ParseUserError. No custom methods are needed yet.

hints.body: Error is the standard marker trait for error values.
Because Display and Debug already exist, this implementation can be empty.
The form is impl std::error::Error for ParseUserError {}.

completionExplanation: Implementing Error makes ParseUserError compatible with APIs that accept standard Rust errors. Empty implementations are common when there is no source error to expose.

title: Fold quantities into a total
scenario: Inventory reporting often needs a single total count across many items.
instructions: Implement total_quantity with iter().fold. Add each item quantity into the accumulator.

hints.body: fold starts with an initial accumulator value.
The accumulator and the item reference are the closure arguments.
The initial total should be 0.

completionExplanation: fold makes the accumulator mechanics explicit: start at zero, visit each item, and return the next total. That gives later reducer logic a clear place to grow.

title: Map log levels with match
scenario: The viewer needs a stable severity number for each known log level.
instructions: Keep the borrowed log entry and view code, then add LogLevel variants Info, Warn, and Error. Implement severity with a match on level.

hints.body: Enums pair well with match because every known case can be handled directly.
The severity function should return a small integer for each variant.
A match expression is clearer than string comparisons for typed log levels.

completionExplanation: Pattern matching keeps the mapping exhaustive and visible. Adding a new log level later will naturally point maintainers at this decision.

title: Start the builder from Default
scenario: Callers should be able to create an empty builder using the standard Default trait.
instructions: Derive Default for RequestBuilder. Option fields already default to None, so the empty builder does not need a manual impl.

hints.body: Default should produce the same empty state every time.
Option<T> implements Default by returning None.
Add Default to the derive list on RequestBuilder.

completionExplanation: Deriving Default is idiomatic when every field already has the desired default. Option fields make the empty builder state explicit by starting as None.

title: Model an optional timeout
scenario: Some deployments need a request timeout while others should inherit the platform default. The type should make that optionality explicit.
instructions: Add timeout_seconds: Option<u64> to Config. Use None in Default.

hints.body: Option<T> represents a value that may be present or absent.
A timeout expressed in seconds can be a u64.
None is clearer than a magic value such as 0 when no timeout is configured.

completionExplanation: Option makes absence part of the type instead of encoding it with a sentinel value. Callers must handle both configured and not-configured cases.

title: Choose clarity over clever chains
scenario: Iterator chains are powerful, but a dense chain can hide business rules. This helper should be easy to scan.
instructions: Refactor the existing reorder_notes helper into a clearer shape. Use a small loop and named intermediate values so the inventory priority rule is obvious.

hints.body: The goal is not always fewer lines.
Name a boolean such as is_inventory_note if that makes the rule clearer.
A loop is idiomatic when each step deserves a name.

completionExplanation: Idiomatic Rust values clarity over using an iterator chain everywhere. The best shape is the one that makes the business rule easiest to verify.

title: Allow borrowed or owned messages
scenario: Most log messages can be borrowed, but some normalized messages need owned text. Cow supports both without forcing allocation every time.
instructions: Keep the existing log view and level code, then define LogMessage<'a> with text: Cow<'a, str>. Import Cow from std::borrow.

hints.body: Cow means clone-on-write and lives in std::borrow.
The borrowed form for text is str, so the field type is Cow<'a, str>.
This is useful when most data can be borrowed but a few cases need ownership.

completionExplanation: Cow lets the API accept borrowed text by default while still supporting owned normalized messages. It avoids unnecessary allocation without giving up flexibility.

title: Validate builder output
scenario: A builder can be incomplete. build should report missing required fields instead of creating a bad Request.
instructions: Add a path setter, define BuildError with MissingMethod and MissingPath, and implement build so missing required fields return the matching error. A built Request should keep body as None for now.

hints.body: The build method is where optional builder state becomes required request state.
ok_or can turn an Option into a Result with a specific missing-field error.
Bind method and path first, then return Ok(Request { method, path, body: None }).

completionExplanation: Returning Result keeps invalid builder states from silently becoming invalid requests. Specific missing-field variants tell callers exactly what still needs to be set.

title: Preserve the ID parse source
scenario: Invalid IDs come from ParseIntError. Keeping that source error helps diagnostics without giving up the typed ParseUserError API.
instructions: Change InvalidId so it stores ParseIntError. Update parse_user to pass the parse error through map_err instead of discarding it, and expose it from Error::source.

hints.body: Tuple variants can carry the original error value.
map_err can pass a function or variant constructor when the types line up.
After the change, InvalidId should look like InvalidId(ParseIntError), and Error::source should return that inner error.

completionExplanation: The parse error now keeps its original source and exposes it through Error::source. That keeps the high-level error useful while preserving details for logging and debugging.

title: Return validation errors with Result
scenario: Configuration validation should explain what is wrong instead of returning a bare boolean.
instructions: Keep the Config fields from earlier lessons, including use_tls and timeout_seconds. Add ConfigError variants EmptyServiceUrl and ZeroConnections. Add Config::validate(&self) -> Result<(), ConfigError>.

hints.body: Result<(), ConfigError> says success has no extra value, but failure is typed.
Use one variant for an empty URL and one for zero connections.
Return Ok(()) only after all validation checks pass.

completionExplanation: Result gives callers a success or a specific reason for failure. That is more useful than a boolean once validation can fail in multiple ways.

title: Convert ParseIntError with From
scenario: The parser has one low-level error that should become a ParseUserError. A From implementation makes that conversion reusable.
instructions: Implement From<ParseIntError> for ParseUserError by returning ParseUserError::InvalidId(error). Then use ? for the ID parse in parse_user.

hints.body: From<T> defines fn from(value: T) -> Self.
The body only needs to wrap the ParseIntError in the InvalidId variant.
This conversion lets parse_user use ? instead of spelling out map_err at the call site.

completionExplanation: From centralizes the low-level to high-level error conversion. Call sites can ask for ParseUserError without rewriting the wrapping logic each time.

title: Make Inventory iterable
scenario: A wrapper type can expose natural iteration while still owning its internal Vec.
instructions: The starter already defines Inventory with a private Vec<Item>. Implement IntoIterator for Inventory so consuming the wrapper yields Item values.

hints.body: Keep Inventory's items field private; the iterator can consume it from inside the impl.
Vec<Item> already has an into_iter method that consumes the vector.
The associated IntoIter type can reuse std::vec::IntoIter<Item>.

completionExplanation: IntoIterator lets Inventory participate in for loops and iterator adapters without exposing its internal field as public API.

title: Check log filtering behavior
scenario: The log viewer should keep high-priority entries and discard routine information when asked for alerts.
instructions: The starter already defines AlertEntry and alert_messages. Fill in the two test TODO blocks with checks for keeping Error and Warn entries while excluding Info entries.

hints.body: Use the provided entry helper to build a small array of alert entries.
The first test should prove that warning and error messages are retained in order.
The second test should make the Info exclusion explicit.

completionExplanation: Focused behavior checks document the alert filtering contract. They make the important inclusion and exclusion rules visible for the next reader.

title: Convert RawRequest with TryFrom
scenario: A small input DTO can be converted into a validated Request using the same fallible conversion pattern as parser code.
instructions: The starter defines RawRequest with optional method, path, and body. Implement TryFrom<RawRequest> for Request, use BuildError as the associated error type, and preserve the optional body.

hints.body: TryFrom is appropriate because RawRequest may be missing required data.
The associated Error type should be BuildError.
Use ok_or to extract required fields, then move value.body into the Request.

completionExplanation: TryFrom turns a possibly incomplete input type into a validated domain type. The conversion API clearly communicates that failure is possible.

title: Borrow setting keys
scenario: A lookup helper should not take ownership of a key just to compare it with stored names.
instructions: Keep the Config, ConfigError, and validate behavior from earlier lessons. Change get_setting so key is borrowed as &str and the returned value is borrowed from settings as Option<&str>.

hints.body: The function only needs to read key, not store it.
Returning &str can borrow from the String already inside settings.
as_str converts a String reference into a string slice.

completionExplanation: Borrowing keeps the lookup API lightweight. The caller keeps its key, and the returned value can point into the existing settings data.

title: Sort items by borrowed comparison
scenario: Before rendering an inventory table, the service wants stable alphabetical ordering by item name without allocating temporary sort keys.
instructions: Add sort_by_name without cloning item names. Sort the mutable slice by comparing each item's borrowed name.

hints.body: Slices expose in-place sorting methods.
For String fields, sort_by with name.cmp(&other.name) avoids allocating cloned keys.
The comparison closure receives two item references: left and right.

completionExplanation: Sorting by borrowed comparison keeps the ordering rule close to the sort call without cloning every String. That is usually the better API choice when the key is already stored in each item.

title: Document the request builder
scenario: The request API is small enough that one doc example can show its intended usage better than a long paragraph.
instructions: Add a short doc comment example above RequestBuilder. Show creating a builder, setting method and path, and checking the Result from build without unwrap or expect.

hints.body: Doc comments use /// before the item they describe.
A compact example should show the normal path and assert on the Result instead of unwrapping it.
Keep the example focused on how a caller should read the API.

completionExplanation: A doc example teaches the API at the point of use. Showing Result-aware usage keeps the documentation aligned with fallible, production-style builder APIs.

title: Parse User with TryFrom
scenario: Parsing a User from text is a fallible conversion. TryFrom<&str> gives callers a standard API for that operation.
instructions: Implement TryFrom<&str> for User. Set type Error to ParseUserError and delegate to parse_user.

hints.body: TryFrom uses an associated Error type.
The conversion method is try_from(value: &str) -> Result<Self, Self::Error>.
Delegate to the existing parser so this lesson adds a standard API without duplicating parser logic.

completionExplanation: TryFrom communicates that parsing can fail and gives callers a standard conversion shape. Delegating keeps one parsing implementation instead of two drifting copies.

title: Write parser behavior checks
scenario: The parser arc ends with examples that document success and failure behavior for future maintainers.
instructions: Fill in the two TODO blocks with one success check and one failure check. Use assert_eq! and matches! or a match expression; do not use unwrap or expect.

hints.body: A useful success example compares the full Ok(User { ... }) value.
A useful failure example checks the exact error variant, not just that an error happened.
matches!(result, Err(ParseUserError::MissingEmail)) is a compact shape for the failure case.

completionExplanation: Focused behavior checks turn the parser contract into executable examples for humans and future tooling. They show both the happy path and the important failure shape.

title: Hide EmailAddress internals
scenario: An email address should eventually be validated before callers can create one. Start by making the domain type own its text without exposing the field.
instructions: Replace the TODO with a private value: String field on EmailAddress.

hints.body: The field should live inside EmailAddress, but callers should not set it directly.
Use a named struct field with type String. Leave off pub so construction can be controlled later.
A canonical solution keeps the representation private.

completionExplanation: A private field lets EmailAddress own its text while reserving construction for validating APIs. That is the first step toward making invalid email values harder to represent.

title: Validate EmailAddress with TryFrom
scenario: Raw text from forms and APIs should become an EmailAddress only after validation. This is a fallible conversion.
instructions: Add EmailAddress::as_str, then implement TryFrom<&str> for EmailAddress. Reject an empty string with Empty and text without @ with MissingAt.

hints.body: The conversion can fail, so the return type should be Result<Self, Self::Error>.
TryFrom has an associated Error type. Set it to EmailValidationError and return Self with a private value on success.
A canonical solution validates before constructing EmailAddress.

completionExplanation: TryFrom makes validation part of the standard conversion API. Callers can use EmailAddress::try_from or try_into without learning a custom constructor name.

title: Name the missing domain case
scenario: An address with @ but no domain should not be accepted. The error type should name that failure instead of reusing a vague variant.
instructions: Add MissingDomain to EmailValidationError and update TryFrom<&str> so text ending with @ returns that variant.

hints.body: The enum should have one variant for each failure mode callers may handle differently.
Use split_once('@') or another clear check to see whether any text exists after @.
A canonical solution adds a variant and checks the domain part before constructing the value.

completionExplanation: A distinct MissingDomain variant keeps validation errors programmatic. Callers can match the exact case without parsing message text.

title: Format EmailAddress with Display
scenario: Callers should be able to print an EmailAddress without reaching into its private field.
instructions: Implement std::fmt::Display for EmailAddress by writing the stored email text.

hints.body: Display receives &self and a formatter. It should not consume the domain value.
Use write!(f, "{}", self.value) inside fmt.
A canonical solution delegates formatting to the private string.

completionExplanation: Display exposes the public text representation without making the field public or adding unchecked construction paths. The type keeps its validation boundary while still fitting normal formatting APIs.

title: Format validation errors
scenario: EmailValidationError is useful to match on, but logs and UI messages need human-readable text.
instructions: Implement Display for EmailValidationError and then implement std::error::Error for it.

hints.body: Keep the enum variants programmatic. Display is where human-facing text belongs.
Match on self inside fmt. The Error implementation can be empty once Debug and Display exist.
A canonical solution gives every variant its own message and marks the enum as a standard error.

completionExplanation: Display keeps user-facing text separate from programmatic error variants. Implementing Error lets the validation error fit standard Rust error APIs.

title: Parse EmailAddress with FromStr
scenario: Once a domain type can validate &str input, callers also expect the standard string parsing API.
instructions: Implement std::str::FromStr for EmailAddress. Reuse EmailAddress::try_from instead of duplicating validation.

hints.body: FromStr is another standard fallible conversion shape for string input.
Set type Err = EmailValidationError, then delegate parse to EmailAddress::try_from.
A canonical solution keeps TryFrom as the single validation implementation.

completionExplanation: FromStr gives callers the familiar "text".parse::<EmailAddress>() API. Delegating to TryFrom avoids two validation implementations that can drift apart.

title: Define a basic Money struct
scenario: The system needs to represent monetary amounts in integer minor units. To prevent bugs, we must group the numeric amount and the currency type together into a single domain type instead of passing loose primitives around.
instructions: Define a public Money struct with private amount (u64 minor units) and currency (Currency) fields.

hints.body: Define pub struct Money.
The fields should be private: amount: u64 and currency: Currency. Do not add pub before them.
A canonical solution defines the Money struct with private fields.

completionExplanation: The Money struct keeps the amount and currency together as one domain value. Private fields prevent callers from mutating the representation directly, which gives later constructors and methods a single place to preserve invariants.

title: Add supported Currency variants
scenario: The payment gateway is expanding. We need to support British Pounds (GBP) in our Currency representation.
instructions: Add Gbp to the Currency enum.

hints.body: Enums are updated by adding new variants separated by commas.
Add Gbp as a variant to the Currency enum.
A canonical solution adds Gbp to the enum.

completionExplanation: Updating the enum extends the supported domain concepts without altering the rest of the type structure.

title: Add a Money constructor and accessors
scenario: Now that Money has private fields, callers need an intentional way to create values and read them back without mutating the representation directly.
instructions: Expose Money::new(amount: u64, currency: Currency) -> Self and accessor methods amount(&self) -> u64 and currency(&self) -> Currency.

hints.body: Put the constructor and accessors inside an impl Money block.
new should return Self. The accessors should borrow self and return copyable values.
A canonical solution keeps the fields private and exposes a narrow public API.

completionExplanation: A constructor and read-only accessors give callers the operations they need while keeping the representation under the type's control. That makes later validation and checked operations possible without changing every call site.

title: Add Money with a typed error
scenario: Callers need to add two money values, but currency mismatches and integer overflow are expected domain failures. Library code should return typed errors instead of panicking.
instructions: Implement Money::checked_add(self, rhs: Self) -> Result<Self, MoneyAddError>. Return CurrencyMismatch for different currencies and AmountOverflow for checked integer overflow.

hints.body: Keep this as a method returning Result instead of implementing an operator that can panic.
Check currency equality first, then use u64::checked_add to combine the minor-unit amounts safely.
A canonical solution returns programmatic errors for both domain and arithmetic failures.

completionExplanation: Fallible domain operations should make failure visible in the type signature. A Result keeps currency mismatches and overflow recoverable and testable, while avoiding panics in library code.

title: Convert a decimal string to Money
scenario: Forms pass prices as decimal strings such as "19.99". To avoid float rounding bugs, parse those strings into integer minor units with explicit errors and checked arithmetic.
instructions: Implement TryFrom<&str> for Money. Assume the parsed amount is USD. Return specific MoneyParseError variants for empty input, invalid digits, too many decimal places, and overflow.

hints.body: Split once on the decimal point so the major and minor parts can be validated separately.
Parse digits into u64, use checked_mul and checked_add, and map each expected failure to a MoneyParseError variant.
A canonical solution parses text without floats and preserves every expected failure as a typed error.

completionExplanation: Parsing money through TryFrom gives callers a standard fallible conversion API. Using integer minor units and checked arithmetic avoids floating-point rounding, overflow surprises, and panics.

title: Implement Display for Money
scenario: The UI needs to render formatted prices like $19.99, €5.00, or £7.25 without exposing Money's private fields.
instructions: Implement std::fmt::Display for Money. Use '$' for USD, '€' for EUR, '£' for GBP, and output cents formatted to two decimal places.

hints.body: Write an impl std::fmt::Display for Money block.
Match on Currency for the symbol, then use amount / 100 and amount % 100 with {:02} for cents.
A canonical solution formats all supported currencies through the Display trait.

completionExplanation: Display provides the standard formatting API while the value object keeps its representation private. Matching every supported currency also prevents enum additions from being silently ignored.

title: Use NonZeroU16 for Port representation
scenario: A port of 0 is invalid for this application. The standard library NonZeroU16 lets the Port type store only non-zero values after construction.
instructions: Define a Port tuple struct wrapping std::num::NonZeroU16 privately. Expose Port::new(value: u16) -> Option<Self> and Port::value(&self) -> u16.

hints.body: Use a tuple struct: pub struct Port(NonZeroU16);
NonZeroU16::new(value) returns Option<NonZeroU16>. Map this into Port with .map(Self), then use get() in the accessor.
A canonical solution wraps NonZeroU16 and exposes only intentional construction and read access.

completionExplanation: Using NonZeroU16 moves the non-zero rule into the type representation after construction. Downstream code can trust Port values and does not need to repeat the zero check.

title: Define and validate a Host value object
scenario: Hosts arrive as borrowed text from configuration and adapters. We want a domain type that rejects empty values and whitespace before storing an owned String.
instructions: Define HostValidationError and a validate_host helper, then define a Host tuple struct with a private String field. Expose Host::as_str(&self) -> &str and implement TryFrom<&str> for Host by reusing validate_host.

hints.body: Put the empty and whitespace checks in validate_host so later conversions can reuse the same rules.
Return Empty for an empty string and InvalidCharacters if the input contains whitespace; allocate only after validation passes.
A canonical solution validates borrowed text before storing an owned string.

completionExplanation: A validated value object keeps raw strings at the boundary. Implementing TryFrom<&str> avoids forcing callers to allocate invalid input, while Host owns the accepted value after validation.

title: Reuse Host validation for owned strings
scenario: Some adapter and configuration APIs hand us owned String values. The domain type should support those call sites without duplicating validation logic.
instructions: Implement TryFrom<String> for Host by reusing the existing validate_host helper. Validate through a borrowed view, then store the original String on success.

hints.body: The validate_host helper already contains the validation rules; do not copy them into a second implementation.
Borrow the String with value.as_str() for validation, then return Ok(Self(value)) so the owned allocation is reused.
A canonical solution keeps one validation function and avoids an unnecessary reallocation.

completionExplanation: Supporting owned input can improve adapter ergonomics, but validation rules should still live in one place. Validating through a borrowed view and then storing the original String keeps the API consistent without an extra allocation.

title: Compose Host and Port into Endpoint
scenario: Hosts and ports travel together as a network endpoint. The composite type should keep the relationship explicit without exposing mutable internals.
instructions: Define an Endpoint struct with private host: Host and port: Port fields. Provide Endpoint::new(host: Host, port: Port) -> Self, host(&self) -> &Host, and port(&self) -> Port.

hints.body: Define Endpoint as a named-field struct, but leave the fields private.
The host accessor can return &Host to avoid cloning; Port is Copy, so its accessor can return Port.
A canonical solution composes existing value objects behind a narrow API.

completionExplanation: Composing validated value objects makes the endpoint relationship explicit. Keeping Endpoint fields private preserves the ability to add endpoint-level rules later without breaking callers.

title: Give Endpoint sensible local defaults
scenario: Most local service connections use host "localhost" and port 8080. The default should be convenient, deterministic, and free of unwrap or expect.
instructions: Implement Default for Endpoint using Host::localhost() and Port::LOCAL_DEVELOPMENT. Do not use unwrap, expect, or panic.

hints.body: Use Default only for a known-good local configuration; keep fallible parsing at external boundaries.
The starter already gives a known-good Host::localhost() constructor and Port::LOCAL_DEVELOPMENT constant. Compose those in default().
A canonical solution builds defaults from named trusted constants without unwrap or expect.

completionExplanation: Default is useful for known local configuration, but it should not normalize unwraps in library code. Trusted constants and explicit constructors keep the code deterministic without hiding failure paths.

title: Implement Display for Endpoint
scenario: Connection logs and configuration output need endpoint formatting in host:port style without exposing Endpoint internals.
instructions: Implement Display for Endpoint by formatting self.host().as_str() and self.port().value() as host:port.

hints.body: Implement std::fmt::Display for Endpoint.
Use the public accessors: self.host().as_str() and self.port().value().
A canonical solution formats the composite through its public API.

completionExplanation: Display gives the composite type a standard textual representation while callers and formatting code continue to use the public API instead of reaching into private fields.

title: Deserialize a raw register-user DTO
scenario: An HTTP adapter receives partial, untrusted data. Start by representing that boundary data separately from the domain command.
instructions: Define RegisterUserDto with public email and display_name Option<String> fields, and derive serde::Deserialize so JSON boundary input can create it directly.

files.content: // TODO: define the raw adapter DTO for registering a user.

hints.body: DTOs model transport input, so public fields are acceptable at the boundary.
Option<String> lets serde represent missing JSON fields as None.
Derive Deserialize on the DTO, not on the domain command.

completionExplanation: The adapter DTO can now be built from JSON while still keeping untrusted boundary data separate from validated domain data.

title: Keep the validated command serde-free
scenario: The application layer should receive a command type, not raw optional DTO fields. Keep command fields private and expose read-only accessors.
instructions: Keep RegisterUserDto active. Add RegisterUserCommand with private email and display_name fields, a constructor, and borrowed accessors. Do not derive serde traits on the command.

hints.body: DTOs and commands serve different layers.
The command owns validated data but exposes it through borrowed accessors.
Keep Deserialize on RegisterUserDto only.

completionExplanation: The DTO stays a boundary shape, while the command is a domain/application shape with private state and a narrow API.

title: Convert JSON DTOs into commands with TryFrom
scenario: A raw DTO can be missing required fields. TryFrom gives the adapter a standard fallible conversion into the application command.
instructions: Add RegisterUserValidationError and implement TryFrom<RegisterUserDto> for RegisterUserCommand. Return typed errors for missing email or display_name fields.

hints.body: Deserialize gets data into the DTO; TryFrom moves it into validated application data.
Use ok_or for missing Option fields.
The domain command should still have no serde derive.

completionExplanation: The boundary conversion is now explicit and fallible, with missing JSON fields mapped into typed validation errors.

title: Trim DTO fields before command creation
scenario: Boundary strings often contain whitespace. Normalize only after checking the DTO field exists, then reject empty normalized values explicitly.
instructions: Update TryFrom<RegisterUserDto> so it trims both strings and returns EmptyEmail or EmptyDisplayName when a trimmed field is empty.

hints.body: Normalize at the boundary before constructing the command.
Trim borrowed string slices first, then allocate only once for accepted values.
Add EmptyEmail and EmptyDisplayName to the validation enum.

completionExplanation: The DTO conversion now performs boundary normalization without leaking whitespace-only values into the command.

title: Deserialize and validate a bulk DTO
scenario: Batch adapter input needs useful error context. Preserve the row index when one nested DTO cannot become a command.
instructions: Add BulkRegisterDto with serde::Deserialize, BulkRegisterCommand, and BulkRegisterError. Implement TryFrom<BulkRegisterDto>, rejecting empty batches and reporting the index of the first invalid nested DTO.

hints.body: The bulk DTO is another transport shape, so it can derive Deserialize.
Convert each nested DTO through the single-user TryFrom implementation.
Use enumerate so InvalidUser can report the failing index.

completionExplanation: Bulk conversion now reuses the single DTO boundary rules and adds batch-level errors without duplicating validation.

title: Serialize an outbound DTO from a domain event
scenario: Outbound adapters also need explicit boundaries. A domain event can become a transport DTO through an infallible From implementation.
instructions: Add UserRegistered and UserRegisteredDto. Derive serde::Serialize for the outbound DTO and implement From<UserRegistered> for UserRegisteredDto by stringifying user_id and moving email.

hints.body: Outbound DTOs are also boundary types, so deriving Serialize belongs there.
Keep the domain event free of serde derives.
Use From because this conversion cannot fail.

completionExplanation: Inbound and outbound serde now live at the transport boundary, while command and event domain types remain independent from JSON details.

title: Wrap order lines behind a slice API
scenario: A collection wrapper can own a Vec while keeping later invariants and iteration APIs under one type.
instructions: Define OrderLines with a private Vec<OrderLine>, a constructor, len, is_empty, and as_slice so callers can inspect lines without taking ownership of the Vec.

files.content: // TODO: wrap order lines in an API-owned collection type.

hints.body: Keep the Vec private so the wrapper can protect future invariants.
Return a slice for read-only access instead of cloning or exposing the Vec.
Add as_slice alongside len and is_empty.

completionExplanation: OrderLines now owns its representation while exposing a cheap borrowed view for callers.

title: Expose borrowed iteration explicitly
scenario: Callers often need to inspect a collection wrapper without consuming it. A borrowed iterator exposes a view without leaking fields.
instructions: Add OrderLines::iter(&self) -> std::slice::Iter<'_, OrderLine>. Keep as_slice so callers can choose either a slice or an iterator.

hints.body: iter should borrow self; it should not clone the Vec.
The item type should be &OrderLine.
Delegate to self.lines.iter().

completionExplanation: The wrapper now supports idiomatic borrowed iteration without exposing its inner Vec.

title: Consume a wrapper with owned IntoIterator
scenario: When callers are done with a wrapper, IntoIterator should let them move owned values out naturally.
instructions: Implement IntoIterator for OrderLines, yielding owned OrderLine values through std::vec::IntoIter<OrderLine>.

hints.body: Owned IntoIterator should consume self.
The item type is OrderLine, not &OrderLine.
Move the inner Vec into its own iterator.

completionExplanation: Callers can now choose ownership transfer with for line in lines when they are done with the wrapper.

title: Borrow a wrapper directly in for loops
scenario: For loops over &OrderLines should borrow each line instead of consuming the wrapper.
instructions: Implement IntoIterator for &OrderLines, yielding &OrderLine values and delegating to OrderLines::iter.

hints.body: This implementation makes for line in &lines work.
The item type should be a shared borrow.
Delegate to self.iter() so the iterator logic stays in one place.

completionExplanation: OrderLines now behaves like standard collections in borrowed for loops.

title: Mutate through wrapper iterators
scenario: Some update operations should borrow the wrapper mutably and edit each line in place without exposing the Vec field.
instructions: Add iter_mut and implement IntoIterator for &mut OrderLines, yielding &mut OrderLine values without exposing the inner Vec.

hints.body: Mutable iteration should borrow the wrapper mutably.
Yield &mut OrderLine so callers can update line fields in place.
Delegate the IntoIterator implementation to iter_mut.

completionExplanation: The wrapper supports controlled in-place updates while preserving ownership of the collection.

title: Validate and drain collection wrappers
scenario: An order without lines is invalid. TryFrom<Vec<OrderLine>> can enforce that invariant at the wrapper boundary.
instructions: Keep the iterator API active. Add drain(&mut self) and implement TryFrom<Vec<OrderLine>> for OrderLines, returning OrderLinesError::Empty for an empty Vec.

hints.body: TryFrom is the right API when construction can fail.
drain gives callers owned values while leaving the wrapper empty.
Use the existing constructor after the non-empty check passes.

completionExplanation: OrderLines now has a complete collection-shaped API plus a fallible constructor for the non-empty invariant.

title: Derive a typed config error with thiserror
scenario: A configuration loader can fail for different reasons. Start with an enum that keeps those reasons programmatic.
instructions: Define ConfigLoadError with MissingEnvironment, InvalidPort, and FileRead variants. Derive thiserror::Error and give every variant a concise #[error(...)] message.

files.content: // TODO: define ConfigLoadError with thiserror.

hints.body: Use thiserror::Error instead of handwritten Display and Error implementations.
Place one #[error("...")] attribute on each variant.
Keep variants semantic; messages are presentation.

completionExplanation: ConfigLoadError is a normal typed Rust error without repetitive formatting boilerplate.

title: Separate error kinds from error messages
scenario: Typed errors are for programs; Display provides stable human-readable messages at the boundary.
instructions: Keep the derived thiserror messages. Add ConfigLoadError::kind(&self) -> &'static str with stable snake_case values for each variant.

hints.body: Display text is for people; a kind value is for logs and metrics.
Match on self and return a static string.
Do not parse the Display message to classify errors.

completionExplanation: The error now has both human-readable text and a stable machine-readable classification.

title: Preserve an I/O error as a source
scenario: When file reading fails, the high-level config error should keep the original std::io::Error as its source.
instructions: Change FileRead into a variant that stores std::io::Error in a source field marked #[source]. Update kind without changing the public error message.

hints.body: thiserror can expose a source without a handwritten Error implementation.
Use a named source field while this lesson focuses on explicit source tracking.
Match FileRead { .. } in kind.

completionExplanation: The top-level config error keeps its stable message while preserving the concrete I/O cause in the standard error chain.

title: Derive mechanical I/O conversion
scenario: A From implementation centralizes how low-level I/O errors become configuration errors and enables ? at call sites.
instructions: Change FileRead to the tuple variant FileRead(#[from] std::io::Error). Let thiserror derive both Error::source and From<std::io::Error>.

hints.body: Use #[from] when conversion is mechanical and loses no semantic information.
A #[from] field is also treated as the source.
No handwritten From implementation is needed.

completionExplanation: I/O failures now convert with ? through a derived From implementation while retaining their source chain.

title: Derive ParseIntError conversion
scenario: Parsing configuration values should keep lower-level parse details while returning the loader error type.
instructions: Change InvalidPort to InvalidPort(#[from] ParseIntError), update kind, and implement parse_port(&str) -> Result<u16, ConfigLoadError> using ?.

hints.body: ParseIntError is the concrete cause of InvalidPort.
Derive From with #[from].
Ok(value.parse()?) keeps the success type explicit.

completionExplanation: Port parsing now preserves the parser error and propagates it through an idiomatic derived conversion.

title: Add context at the application boundary
scenario: Once conversions exist, loader functions can stay small and let ? preserve the typed error boundary.
instructions: Implement load_port with typed ConfigLoadError values. Then add load_port_with_context returning anyhow::Result<u16> and attach "failed to load APP_PORT" context without replacing the typed inner error.

hints.body: Keep ConfigLoadError inside the reusable loader.
Use anyhow::Context only where the application wants operation-level context.
The context should wrap the typed error, not erase it.

completionExplanation: Reusable code returns typed errors, while the application boundary adds operational context for diagnostics.

title: Design a non-exhaustive domain error
scenario: Domain errors should name business-rule failures without mentioning databases, HTTP, or UI details.
instructions: Define CreateOrderError with EmptyOrder and InvalidQuantity. Derive thiserror::Error, add concise messages, and mark the public enum #[non_exhaustive] so new variants remain a compatible API change.

files.content: // TODO: define the public domain error surface.

hints.body: Public error enums often gain variants over time.
Use #[non_exhaustive] to force downstream wildcard handling.
Use thiserror for the Display and Error implementations.

completionExplanation: The domain error is typed, readable, and designed for API evolution.

title: Keep repository failures at their boundary
scenario: Repository failures are infrastructure boundary failures. Keep them distinct from domain rule failures.
instructions: Keep CreateOrderError. Add a separate #[non_exhaustive] RepositoryError with Unavailable and Conflict variants, deriving thiserror::Error instead of mixing infrastructure failures into the domain enum.

hints.body: Repository failures belong to the repository boundary.
Keep domain and persistence concerns in separate enums.
Mark the public repository enum non-exhaustive too.
hints.solutionCode:
use thiserror::Error;

#[non_exhaustive]
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Error, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum CreateOrderError {
    #[error("order must contain at least one line")]
    EmptyOrder,
    #[error("order line quantity must be positive")]
    InvalidQuantity,
}

#[non_exhaustive]
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Error, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum RepositoryError {
    #[error("repository is unavailable")]
    Unavailable,
    #[error("order conflicts with existing data")]
    Conflict,
}

completionExplanation: Domain and repository failures now evolve independently and remain clear at call sites.

title: Compose boundary errors without flattening them
scenario: The application layer can compose domain and repository errors without leaking adapter response details.
instructions: Define #[non_exhaustive] CreateOrderUseCaseError with Domain(CreateOrderError) and Repository(RepositoryError). Derive thiserror::Error and make each wrapped error a transparent source.

hints.body: The use case should preserve which layer failed.
Transparent variants reuse the wrapped error message.
Use #[source] before adding automatic conversions.
hints.solutionCode:
use thiserror::Error;

#[non_exhaustive]
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Error, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum CreateOrderError {
    #[error("order must contain at least one line")]
    EmptyOrder,
    #[error("order line quantity must be positive")]
    InvalidQuantity,
}

#[non_exhaustive]
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Error, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum RepositoryError {
    #[error("repository is unavailable")]
    Unavailable,
    #[error("order conflicts with existing data")]
    Conflict,
}

#[non_exhaustive]
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Error, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum CreateOrderUseCaseError {
    #[error("domain error: {0}")]
    Domain(#[source] CreateOrderError),
    #[error("repository error: {0}")]
    Repository(#[source] RepositoryError),
}

completionExplanation: The application error composes lower-layer failures without converting them into strings or losing their sources.

title: Derive mechanical boundary conversions
scenario: From keeps application orchestration code focused by centralizing conversion from domain errors.
instructions: Change both transparent fields from #[source] to #[from]. Let thiserror derive From<CreateOrderError> and From<RepositoryError> for CreateOrderUseCaseError.

hints.body: These conversions only wrap the original typed error.
Use #[from] when there is no additional policy or context.
The wrapped value remains the standard error source.
hints.solutionCode:
use thiserror::Error;

#[non_exhaustive]
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Error, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum CreateOrderError {
    #[error("order must contain at least one line")]
    EmptyOrder,
    #[error("order line quantity must be positive")]
    InvalidQuantity,
}

#[non_exhaustive]
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Error, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum RepositoryError {
    #[error("repository is unavailable")]
    Unavailable,
    #[error("order conflicts with existing data")]
    Conflict,
}

#[non_exhaustive]
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Error, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum CreateOrderUseCaseError {
    #[error(transparent)]
    Domain(#[from] CreateOrderError),
    #[error(transparent)]
    Repository(#[from] RepositoryError),
}

completionExplanation: Use-case code can now use ? for both lower-layer error types without handwritten conversion boilerplate.

title: Classify retries without parsing messages
scenario: Not every failure should be retried. Classification helpers let application code make decisions without string matching.
instructions: Implement CreateOrderUseCaseError::is_retryable. Return true only for RepositoryError::Unavailable and classify by variants, not by Display text.

hints.body: Retry policy belongs at the application boundary.
Use matches! for the one retryable shape.
Conflicts and domain errors should not be retried automatically.

completionExplanation: Retry behavior is now explicit, typed, and independent from user-facing error wording.

title: Map typed application errors at the adapter edge
scenario: HTTP status codes belong at the adapter edge. Translate typed application errors there instead of putting HTTP concepts in domain code.
instructions: Implement status_code(&CreateOrderUseCaseError) -> u16 at the adapter boundary: 400 for domain errors, 409 for repository conflicts, and 503 for repository unavailability.

hints.body: HTTP status is adapter policy, not a property of the domain error.
Match explicitly on typed variants.
Keep the mapping simple and exhaustive inside the crate.

completionExplanation: The adapter translates application failures into HTTP policy without leaking status codes into domain or repository types.

title: Keep async service types out of the domain
scenario: Domain types should not depend on HTTP, JSON, databases, or logging setup. Start with pure command and value-object types.
instructions: Define a domain module with EmailAddress and RegisterUserCommand. Keep fields private, expose borrowed accessors, and do not import Tokio, Actix, or serde into the domain module.

files.content: // TODO: define framework-free register-user domain types.

hints.body: The domain should not know which runtime or web framework calls it.
Use private owned fields and borrowed accessors.
Keep all imports out of the domain module.

completionExplanation: The core register-user types are runtime-independent and can be reused by HTTP, jobs, or tests.

title: Define an async repository port with Send futures
scenario: Application code depends on domain types and port traits, not concrete database clients.
instructions: Add application types UserId, NewUser, and RepositoryError. Define UserRepository: Send + Sync with methods returning impl Future + Send for email_exists and save, keeping repository details out of domain types.

hints.body: Persistence operations are asynchronous, but a public trait should state the Send bound of each returned future.
Use return-position impl Future<Output = Result<...>> + Send in the trait.
Concrete implementations can still use async fn.

completionExplanation: The application owns an async persistence contract whose returned futures are explicitly safe to move between runtime worker threads.

title: Implement an async use case over the port
scenario: A use case coordinates domain rules and ports. It should be generic over the port trait so infrastructure stays replaceable.
instructions: Add RegisterUserError and implement async register_user over UserRepository. Check for an existing email, then save NewUser. Map repository conflicts to DuplicateEmail and propagate other typed repository errors.

hints.body: Await each repository operation at the application boundary.
Keep DuplicateEmail as an application-level outcome.
Do not convert repository errors into strings.

completionExplanation: The use case coordinates asynchronous persistence while staying generic over the repository implementation.

title: Deserialize adapter input at the edge
scenario: Adapter DTOs should be converted into domain commands before use-case logic runs.
instructions: Add an adapters module with serde-deserializable RegisterUserRequest and typed RequestError. Implement TryFrom<RegisterUserRequest> for RegisterUserCommand, trimming and validating input before it enters the use case.

hints.body: Serde belongs on the adapter DTO, not on the command.
Trim before constructing domain values.
Return RequestError before calling application code.

completionExplanation: JSON-shaped input is converted into framework-free domain data at the adapter edge.

title: Share an in-memory repository safely
scenario: Infrastructure implements application ports. The application trait stays the dependency boundary.
instructions: Add InMemoryUserRepository backed by Arc<tokio::sync::RwLock<_>>. Implement UserRepository with read locking for lookups and write locking for atomic duplicate checking and insertion.

hints.body: Arc allows cheap repository clones to share one state.
Use a read lock for email_exists.
Repeat duplicate detection under the write lock so concurrent saves stay atomic.

completionExplanation: The infrastructure adapter supports concurrent async access without leaking synchronization primitives through the repository port.

title: Add timeout policy and an Actix handler
scenario: A handler adapter should translate request DTOs, call the use case, and return response data without pushing HTTP concepts into domain code.
instructions: Add register_user_with_timeout using tokio::time::timeout and a TimedOut error. Then add a thin Actix handler using web::Data, web::Json, HttpResponse, and Responder. Keep HTTP status mapping in adapters and the use case free of Actix types.

hints.body: Timeout is application policy around the use-case future.
The handler should only extract inputs, call adapter/application code, and build an HTTP response.
Do not pass web::Json or HttpResponse into domain or application modules.

completionExplanation: The arc ends with cancellation-aware async application code and a thin Actix boundary that preserves module ownership.

title: Emit a structured tracing event
scenario: Structured logging starts with events that have names and levels instead of ad-hoc strings.
instructions: Implement log_request_received(request_id: &str) with tracing::info!. Record event_name = "request.received" and request_id as structured fields instead of formatting them into the message.

files.content: // TODO: emit a structured request event with tracing.

hints.body: Structured fields belong before the message in the tracing macro.
Use %request_id to record its Display representation.
Keep the message constant so fields remain queryable.

completionExplanation: The first event uses real tracing metadata instead of a custom log-record struct or interpolated string.

title: Record consistent request fields
scenario: Useful logs carry fields such as request_id and attempt count so operators can filter and correlate events.
instructions: Add log_request_started with request_id, optional user_id, and attempt parameters. Emit event_name, request_id, user_id, and attempt as separate tracing fields.

hints.body: Use the same field names across events.
Record anonymous when no user id exists.
Keep numeric values as numbers instead of formatting them into text.

completionExplanation: Request metadata is now queryable by stable field names across tracing backends.

title: Redact secrets before they reach tracing
scenario: Logging helpers should make accidental secret exposure difficult. A wrapper can intentionally hide sensitive values when formatted.
instructions: Define Secret as a borrowed wrapper and implement Display so it always writes [redacted]. Add log_authentication_attempt that records the Secret through its Display implementation.

hints.body: Redaction should happen in the type, not at every call site.
Store a borrow so logging does not clone sensitive data.
Never expose the wrapped secret through Display or Debug output.

completionExplanation: Sensitive values now have a logging-safe representation that prevents accidental plaintext formatting.

title: Create a request span for async work
scenario: A span carries context shared by multiple events. Request-scoped fields should not be rebuilt by every call site.
instructions: Implement request_span(request_id, path) -> tracing::Span with tracing::info_span!. Name it "http.request" and record request_id and path fields so async work can enter or instrument the span.

hints.body: Spans represent work with a beginning and end.
Return the Span so callers can enter it or instrument a future.
Keep request context on the span instead of repeating it in every message.

completionExplanation: Request-scoped context can now propagate through synchronous or asynchronous work with a real tracing span.

title: Record typed error kinds as fields
scenario: Logs should capture error category fields without relying on fragile message parsing.
instructions: Define ErrorKind with a stable as_str mapping. Add log_request_error that emits request_id and error.kind as structured fields without logging a full secret-bearing error value.

hints.body: Use a typed enum so call sites cannot invent arbitrary error kinds.
Map variants to stable lowercase strings.
Log the classification separately from the human message.

completionExplanation: Error telemetry now carries a stable machine-readable classification without dumping internal error details.

title: Emit a boundary outcome event
scenario: Domain code should stay free of logging setup. Application boundaries can create events with meaningful fields.
instructions: Implement register_attempt_event with request_id, optional user_id, and success. Emit a tracing event named "register.attempt" and keep every value in a structured field.

hints.body: Boundary events should describe outcomes, not restate implementation details.
Keep the event name stable.
Record success as a bool so backends can filter it directly.

completionExplanation: The logging arc now uses spans and structured tracing events with stable fields, typed error kinds, and secret redaction.

title: Define a property-friendly Percentage type
scenario: A percentage should not be represented as any arbitrary integer throughout the codebase.
instructions: Define Percentage, PercentageError, value, and TryFrom<u16>. Accept values from 0 through 100 and reject larger values without panicking.

files.content: // TODO: define Percentage and its range invariant.

hints.body: Store only valid values in the private u8 field.
Validate before casting from u16.
Return a typed OutOfRange error.

completionExplanation: Percentage establishes a compact invariant that can be tested with examples and generated inputs.

title: Write table-driven valid examples
scenario: Several valid boundary cases should be easy to scan. A table-driven test keeps inputs and expected values together.
instructions: Add a #[cfg(test)] module with one test that loops over valid input/expected pairs for 0, 50, and 100.

hints.body: Keep cases as data rather than repeating assertions.
Include both boundaries and a representative middle value.
Map Percentage to its primitive value for concise assertions.

completionExplanation: One readable test now covers several named points in the valid input partition.

title: Write table-driven invalid examples
scenario: Invalid cases deserve precise tests too. Table-driven tests make it clear which inputs should fail.
instructions: Keep the valid table test. Add a second test that loops over several values above 100 and checks PercentageError::OutOfRange.

hints.body: Invalid cases can share one expected error.
Choose values close to and far from the boundary.
Keep the loop assertion explicit.

completionExplanation: The example suite now documents both accepted and rejected input partitions.

title: Test display behavior with a table
scenario: Formatting is public behavior. A small table keeps display expectations obvious.
instructions: Implement Display for Percentage and add a table-driven test for 0%, 50%, and 100%, preserving the existing range tests.

hints.body: Formatting valid Percentage values is infallible.
Reuse TryFrom in tests instead of constructing the private field.
Compare the final String with the expected text.

completionExplanation: Display behavior is covered by the same compact table style as validation behavior.

title: Add an executable Result-aware example
scenario: A doc example should show normal usage and handle fallible APIs without unwrap or expect.
instructions: Add a rustdoc example above Percentage. Show a successful TryFrom call using ? and assert the value without using unwrap.

hints.body: A hidden Ok type lets the doctest use ?.
Use the public API exactly as downstream callers would.
Keep the example short and executable.

completionExplanation: The public documentation now demonstrates fallible construction with an executable, idiomatic Result example.

title: Combine named cases with property tests
scenario: Named cases make failing table-driven tests easier to diagnose and easier for humans to scan.
instructions: Replace repeated table boilerplate with a small macro_rules! helper that keeps case names visible. Add proptest properties covering every value in 0..=100 and every value above 100.

hints.body: Keep the macro local to tests and focused on readability.
Example cases document business-relevant points.
Property tests complement examples by covering whole input ranges.

completionExplanation: The final test suite combines readable named examples, executable documentation, and generated invariant coverage without hiding domain behavior behind macro complexity.
